teachingPoint: Form schema publishing uses Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) with state transitions: DRAFT → PUBLISHED. Once published, the schema is immutable — no field modifications allowed. Field-level validation rules must be defined in the schema before publish. After publish, only new versions can be created; the published version never changes. This prevents drift between published contracts and client-side rendering logic that depends on schema structure.
positiveExample: Schema state: DRAFT. storeDocumentWithOCC(schema, version=1, expectedVersion=0). Schema moves to PUBLISHED. Later attempt: updateDocument(schema.publishedFields) fails because updateVersion > publishedVersion. New version: createSchemaVersion(baseSchemaId, version=2, DRAFT).
negativeExample: Schema published, version=1. Later call: updateDocument(schema, {fields: modifiedFields}, version=1). Field structure changes after clients already cached published version. Clients render old field structure, server validates new structure. Form submission fails with validation error.
discriminatingConstraint: T629 FormSchemaPublisher owns DRAFT→PUBLISHED state transition via OCC. Once PUBLISHED, schema.fields is immutable (no updates to published version allowed). New schema versions created as separate documents. Field validation rules must exist in schema before publish.

teachingPoint: Form submission processing validates the incoming submission data against the published form schema. Every field in the submission must match the field definition in the published schema (type, required, constraints). The submitter's tenantId (from AsyncLocalStorage) MUST match the form.tenantId. This is a BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) check — the submitter owns the form only if tenant matches. Validation failures return DataProcessResult.success (not failure) with validation errors in the data field for client display.
positiveExample: submitterTenantId = cls.get(TENANT_CONTEXT_KEY).tenantId. submitterTenantId === form.tenantId → proceed. Validate submission.field[i] against schema.fields[i].type, required, constraints. Invalid? return DataProcessResult.success({valid: false, errors: [...]}).
negativeExample: Accepting submission without checking form.tenantId vs submitterTenantId. Submitter from tenant-A submitting to form owned by tenant-B (BOLA bypass). Comparing submission data against local cached schema instead of published schema version — cached version may be outdated.
discriminatingConstraint: T630 FormSubmissionProcessor performs BOLA check: submitterTenantId === form.tenantId (from ALS, never from payload). Validates against published schema only (immutable version). Validation errors returned via DataProcessResult.success with error array (DNA-4).

teachingPoint: Automation rules are conditionally dispatched via a rule engine. Each rule has a trigger condition (e.g., formSubmitted, fieldValue=X) and an action (emit event, call webhook, transform data). Dispatch uses SETNX atomicity for execution locks — only one concurrent execution of a rule per submission. If lock acquired, rule executes to completion. If lock held, new execution waits or returns idempotency response. Conditional branching based on submission data allows multi-path automation (if field A > 100, branch X; else branch Y).
positiveExample: rule.trigger = {type: 'formSubmitted', formId: '...'}. rule.condition = {field: 'amount', operator: '>', value: 100}. SETNX(rule-exec-lock:{submissionId}:{ruleId}). If lock acquired: evaluate condition, dispatch action. Store execution record (DATA-8 outbox pattern).
negativeExample: No execution lock — concurrent submissions trigger rule N times. No conditional branching — all automation paths execute regardless of submission data. Rule dispatch happens before submission is stored (violates outbox pattern, DATA-8).
discriminatingConstraint: T631 AutomationDispatcher owns rule dispatch. SETNX(rule-exec-lock:{submissionId}:{ruleId}) ensures single execution per rule per submission. Conditional branching via rule.condition evaluation. Store submission before dispatching (outbox pattern, DNA-8).

teachingPoint: Submission analytics are append-only, capturing aggregated metrics (submission count, average field values, error rate) at tenant scope. Analytics exclude PII fields (email, phone, ssn) — these are never indexed or aggregated. Analytics records are immutable once stored (insert only, no updates). Aggregation windows (daily, hourly) partition data for efficient time-series queries. Tenant-scoped aggregation prevents cross-tenant data visibility.
positiveExample: After submission validation: extract analytics-safe fields (status, fieldCount, validationErrors). Store in xiigen-submission-analytics with tenant scope. Exclude: email, phone, ssn, password. Query: searchDocuments('xiigen-submission-analytics', {tenantId, dateWindow: 'TODAY', formId}). Aggregate: {submitCount: N, avgFieldCount: M, errorRate: P%}.
negativeExample: Storing email/phone/ssn in analytics index. Querying analytics without tenant filter (cross-tenant data leak). Updating analytics records (lost immutability guarantee). No date partitioning — time-series query on huge dataset slows down.
discriminatingConstraint: T632 SubmissionAnalyticsCollector stores append-only analytics with PII exclusion. Tenant-scoped aggregation. No email, phone, ssn, password in analytics index. Date-windowed partitioning for efficient queries.

description: Form schema publishing using OCC to manage DRAFT→PUBLISHED state transitions. Published schemas are immutable; new versions created separately. Field-level validation rules defined before publish. Prevents schema drift between client and server expectations.
codeSnippet: const schema = await db.searchDocuments('xiigen-form-schemas', {schemaId}); if (schema.status !== 'DRAFT') throw; await db.storeDocumentWithOCC(schema, version, expectedVersion); schema.status = 'PUBLISHED';
ironRules: IR-1: OCC via storeDocumentWithOCC(schema, version, expectedVersion)
IR-2: Published schema.fields immutable — no updates allowed
IR-3: Field validation rules must exist before publish

description: Form submission validation against published schema with BOLA check. Submitter tenantId must match form.tenantId. Validation errors returned as success response (not failure). Prevents unauthorized submission to forms in other tenants.
codeSnippet: const submitterTenantId = cls.get(TENANT_CONTEXT_KEY).tenantId; if (submitterTenantId !== form.tenantId) return failure('UNAUTHORIZED'); const validationErrors = validateAgainstPublishedSchema(data); return success({valid: errors.length === 0, errors});
ironRules: IR-2: Validate against published schema only

description: Rule engine automation dispatch with SETNX execution locks and conditional branching. Single execution per rule per submission. Multi-path branching based on submission data conditions. Outbox pattern ensures submission stored before dispatch.
codeSnippet: for (const rule of rules) { const lockKey = `rule-exec-lock:${submissionId}:${ruleId}`; const locked = await acquireLock(lockKey); if (!locked) continue; const conditionResult = evaluateCondition(submissionData); const actions = conditionResult ? rule.thenActions : rule.elseActions; for (const action of actions) dispatch(action); }

description: Append-only submission analytics with PII exclusion and tenant-scoped aggregation. Immutable records. Date-windowed partitioning for efficient time-series queries. No email, phone, ssn in analytics index.
codeSnippet: const piiFields = new Set(['email', 'phone', 'ssn', 'password']); const analyticsData = {}; for (const [k, v] of Object.entries(data)) { if (!piiFields.has(k)) analyticsData[k] = v; } await db.storeDocument('xiigen-submission-analytics', analyticsData);
ironRules: IR-1: PII exclusion (email, phone, ssn, password)
